coredata.dc:title: Au modified spindle-shaped cerium phosphate as an efficient co-reaction accelerator to amplify electrochemiluminescence signal of carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive analysis of aflatoxin B1
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Min
Yue, Qi
Fang, Jinglong
Wang, Caihong
Cao, Wei
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description:
In various detection methods of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), false positives and false negatives are common due to the low sensitivity, expensive equipment or improper pretreatment during operation. Here, a sandwich electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor armed with a synergistic co-reaction acceleration strategy was employed for the ultra-sensitive detection of AFB1. Benefiting from the catalytic properties of Ce3+/Ce4+ redox pairs, cerium phosphate@gold (CePO4@Au), for the first time, was introduced into the immunosensor as a new type of co-reaction accelerator, and it significantly improved the ECL intensity of the nitrogen doped hydrazide conjugated carbon dots (NHCDs)-H2O2 system. CePO4@Au has the ability to promote the decomposition of H2O2 and accelerate the formation of O2
•− . The more of O2
•− reacts with NHCDs to produce a stronger and more robust ECL signal response. Furthermore, the spindle-shaped sensing interface formed by oxidation cerium and phosphate has a high loading area and good biocompatibility, and the modification of Au nanoparticles further achieves the stable binding of the capture antibody. Striving for further improvement, Ag modifies Barium titanate (BaTiO3), as the signal carrier with amplification ability, loads with a large number of anodic luminescent carbon quantum dots, were adopted as detection markers for the construction of sandwich ECL biosensors. The co-reaction amplification system achieves high precision quantitative detection of AFB1 in the linear range of 0.01 pg/mL–100 ng/mL, and the detection limit is 9.55 fg/mL. In addition, the constructed biosensor also showed good stability, reproducibility and specificity, with a promising application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Co-reaction acceleration
AFB1
CePO4@Au

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism exploration of CePO4 and NHCDs-H2O2 system
Electrochemical characterization of the construction process
Stability, reproducibility and specificity of biosensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of materials and mechanism of electrochemiluminescence immunosensor.
SEM of CePO4 (A); SEM of CePO4@Au (B); XRD pattern of CePO4 (C); TEM and HRTEM (insert) images of NHCDs (D); TEM image of BaTiO3 (E); XRD pattern of BaTiO3 (F).
Mechanism diagram (A) and (B); Effect picture of CePO4@Au on NHCDs (C).

CV curve diagram under different modification states (A), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (B), (a) GCE/CePO4@Au, (b) GCE, (c) GCE/CePO4@Au/Ab1, (d) GCE/CePO4@Au/Ab1/BSA, (e) GCE/CePO4@Au/Ab1/BSA/NHCDs@BTO@Ag (f) GCE/CePO4@Au/Ab1/BSA/NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2.
Optimized the concentration of CePO4@Au (A), The optimal concentration of NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2 (B), The optimal amount of H2O2 (C), Optimal pH of test solution (D).

The immunosensor responds to ECL of different concentrations of AFB1; Different concentrations of AFB1: (a) 100 ng/mL, (b) 50 ng/mL, (c) 10 ng/mL, (d) 1 ng/mL, (e) 100 pg/mL, (f) 10 pg/mL, (g) 1 pg/mL, (h) 100 fg/mL, (i) 10 fg/mL (A), Calibration chart for AFB1 detection (B), Stability of electrodes with different concentrations (C), Long-term test stability (D), Reproducibility (E), Selectivity (F).

head.ce:title: Au modified spindle-shaped cerium phosphate as an efficient co-reaction accelerator to amplify electrochemiluminescence signal of carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive analysis of aflatoxin B1

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction and Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction and Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction and Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Safety, Binzhou University, Binzhou 256600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemical Engineering and Safety, Binzhou University, Binzhou 256600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In various detection methods of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), false positives and false negatives are common due to the low sensitivity, expensive equipment or improper pretreatment during operation. Here, a sandwich electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor armed with a synergistic co-reaction acceleration strategy was employed for the ultra-sensitive detection of AFB1. Benefiting from the catalytic properties of Ce3+/Ce4+ redox pairs, cerium phosphate@gold (CePO4@Au), for the first time, was introduced into the immunosensor as a new type of co-reaction accelerator, and it significantly improved the ECL intensity of the nitrogen doped hydrazide conjugated carbon dots (NHCDs)-H2O2 system. CePO4@Au has the ability to promote the decomposition of H2O2 and accelerate the formation of O2
•− . The more of O2
•− reacts with NHCDs to produce a stronger and more robust ECL signal response. Furthermore, the spindle-shaped sensing interface formed by oxidation cerium and phosphate has a high loading area and good biocompatibility, and the modification of Au nanoparticles further achieves the stable binding of the capture antibody. Striving for further improvement, Ag modifies Barium titanate (BaTiO3), as the signal carrier with amplification ability, loads with a large number of anodic luminescent carbon quantum dots, were adopted as detection markers for the construction of sandwich ECL biosensors. The co-reaction amplification system achieves high precision quantitative detection of AFB1 in the linear range of 0.01 pg/mL–100 ng/mL, and the detection limit is 9.55 fg/mL. In addition, the constructed biosensor also showed good stability, reproducibility and specificity, with a promising application prospect.

ce:section.ce:para: As people attach great importance to physical health, the requirements for various foods will become higher and higher, such as hygiene, nontoxic, nutrition, etc. However, mycotoxins have been plagued by human health and food safety since humans began to engage in food crop production. Aflatoxins are ubiquitous in various food crops, including grains, nuts and other agricultural products [1]. People's health will be affected when people eat food that is contaminated with aflatoxin [2,3]. AFB1 is one of the most contaminating mycotoxins, and it is mutagenic, teratogenic and carcinogenic [4,5]. In the meantime, it contaminates a lot of food and agricultural products in a particular way. As the main inducing toxin of liver cancer, AFB1 has been listed as the first group of carcinogens by the authoritative organization (International Agency for Research on Cancer IARC) [6,7]. Therefore, the accurate and efficient detection of AFB1 is vital. There are several conventional analytical methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [8], photoelectric immunoassay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent method [9], and so on. Although these analysis methods can detect AFB1, they require complicated procedures, expensive equipment and professional operators, which limits their wide range of applications. Therefore, it is an urgent need to have high-sensitivity, accurate, reliable and low-cost detection methods to detect aflatoxins quickly.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an ideal combination of electrochemical and chemiluminescence reactions. It combines the simplicity, stability and controllability of electrochemical methods with the high sensitivity of chemiluminescence methods. Based on the quantitative relationship between ECL signal and analyte concentration, the way can achieve accurate detection [10–12]. As a relatively practical analytical technique, ECL is widely used to determine industrial and clinical biomolecules. Since Bard et al. first discovered the ECL phenomenon of silicon quantum dots in 2002, many ECL emitters based on quantum materials have been widely used in the biological analysis [11,13,14]. The carbon quantum dots are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with excellent luminescence properties, due to the outstanding advantages such as low cytotoxicity, high stability and good biocompatibility, and they are expected to be widely used in biosensing or imaging [15–19]. However, traditional carbon quantum dots require a high excitation potential to exhibit ECL emission, and this high excitation potential has certain damages to biological systems. Therefore, reducing the excitation potential and enhancing the ECL signal are the keys to the wide application of carbon quantum dots. Yuan et al. innovatively synthesized a new type of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with anodic ECL under low excitation potential in 2019. By using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as the coreactive material, carbon quantum dots can produce powerful ECL emission in solution [20,21]. However, H2O2 is recognized as a low-toxic substance. If it is not completely decomposed, it will do harm to the test substance and affect the test results. Therefore, the complete decomposition of H2O2 is an urgent problem to be solved.

In recent years, rare earth phosphates have received more and more attention from researchers due to their low synthesis temperature, low toxicity, and high quantum yield [22]. Lanthanide phosphates are a kind of important rare earth compounds. Based on the sizeable atomic radius of the lanthanide elements and the 4f electronic characteristics, lanthanide phosphates exhibit many unique characteristics in terms of optics, conductivity, biocompatibility and catalysis [23]. In this work, we synthesized CePO4 using the high affinity of phosphoric acid groups for lanthanide ions, and applied it to the field of ECL for the first time. The excellent morphological characteristics, high thermal stability, suitable acid strength and low toxicity of CePO4 nanomaterials make it have great application potential in many fields [24–30]. The well-dispersed gold nanoparticles on the surface of CePO4 are further modified to improve the conductivity of the material, the electro-catalysis performance and the precise capture of antibodies [31]. As a co-reaction accelerator, CePO4@Au has two main advantages. For one thing, CePO4@Au can promote the decomposition of H2O2 to produce O2 •−. The repeated conversion of Ce4+ and Ce3+ has high electrical and catalytic activity, which can facilitate the production of O2 •− [32–35]. The generated O2 •− reacts with NHCDs• to produce a stronger luminescence signal. For another, when the hexagonal CePO4 is modified on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE), the effective surface area and electron transfer rate of GCE can be significantly increased, and the modification of gold nanoparticles further realizes the stable binding of antibodies. BaTiO3 is often used as a catalyst because of its stability and availability [36]. At the same time, it shows good biocompatibility in cell culture and in vivo application [37–39]. Metal nanoparticles (such as platinum, gold, and silver) can improve the conductivity of semiconductors. Hence, Ag nanoparticles were modified on the surface of BaTiO3 (BTO) to enhance conductivity [40]. The surface of NHCDs contains -NH structure, so it can be adsorbed on the surface of BTO@Ag through the complexation of Ag and -NH structure to provide stable and efficient light emission. Based on the above advantages, we chose BTO@Ag as the signal carrier to immobilize NHCDs and accurately anchored antibody.

In this work, the hydrothermally synthesized hexagonal phase CePO4 was used for the first time in the ECL immunosensor. On this basis, a super sensitive and enhanced sandwich ECL biosensor was successfully developed to detect AFB1 sensitively [41,42]. CePO4@Au was used as an excellent the sensing platform, and NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2 was used as a stable the ECL indicator, aiming to achieve accurate and efficient detection of AFB1. The biosensor has good linearity in the range of 0.01 pg/mL–100 ng/mL, and the detection limit is 9.55 fg/mL.

CePO4 was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The specific process referred to the previous reports, but had some slight modifications [43]. To begin with, 3 mmol Ce(NO3)3•6H2O and 3 mmol (NH4)2HPO4 were respectively dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Under strong stirring, the (NH4)2HPO4 aqueous solution was slowly added dropwise to the Ce(NO3)3•6H2O aqueous solution to form a white precipitate. Equally important, an appropriate amount of 25 wt.% ammonia solution was added dropwise to the mixture and the pH was adjusted to about 10.45. The white precipitate was dissolved to form a transparent and colorless solution. The resulting solution was poured into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon. The autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene was sealed and heated at 150 °C in an oven for 12 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature in the air. After that, the obtained product was collected and washed several times with distilled water and absolute ethanol and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The synthesis of Au NPs was described in detail in the supporting information. CePO4@Au nanoparticles were prepared by electrostatic adsorption method, 0.02 g CePO4 was added to Au NPs and shaken for 24 h to complete the surface modification of CePO4 by gold nanoparticles. After centrifugation and washing, it was dried to obtain CePO4@Au.

The synthesis of BTO was prepared by the previously reported method with slight modifications [44]. First, 5 mmol of tetrabutyl titanate was slowly dropped into 20 mL of diethanolamine. Second, under continuous stirring, 25 mL of 14.4 mol/L NaOH and 5 mL of 1 mol/L BaCl2 aqueous solution were slowly added to the above diethanolamine solution to form a white precipitate suspension. Third, the suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained product was washed several times with dilute hydrochloric acid, deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 70 °C for 12 h.

Totally, 0.02 g BTO was dispersed in 20 mL of 0.01 mol/L AgNO3 solution. Under continuous stirring, 5 mL of 0.01 mol/L NaBH4 was added to the mixed solution and the stirring was continued for 12 h. Finally, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, then washed with ultrapure water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. BTO@Ag nanocube was obtained.

See the supporting information for the detailed process of NHCDs. A total of 0.05 g BTO@Ag and 0.01 g NHCDs were dispersed in 10 mL deionized water. After shaking for 24 h, they were washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water for several times and dried for standby. The purified NHCDs@BTO@Ag were collected and dispersed in 5 mL of phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) (pH 7.38). Then, 500 μL Ab2 solution (10 μg/mL) was added to NHCDs@BTO@Ag solution, and the incubation process was completed by shaking at 4 °C. After centrifugation, the obtained NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2 bioconjugate was stored at 4 °C for sensor construction.

Scheme 1 revealed the construction process of the ECL immunosensor. Before the electrode was coated, GCE was polished by alumina powder. After washing with ethanol, deionized water, and blow-dry under N2 atmosphere, the pretreated GCE was ready to be used for the first coating of CePO4@Au with 8 μL. After being dried at room temperature, 8 μL of 1 μg/mL Ab1 was dropped onto the electrode surface, incubated at 4 °C and dried. Totally, 3 μL of 1% BSA was dropped on the surface of the modified electrode to block nonspecific active sites and dried at 4 °C. Then, 8 μL aflatoxin was connected to the electrode. Soon after, 8 μL of NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2 biological conjugate solution was dropped onto the modified GCE to complete the construction process. In each step, the unabsorbed material was gently washed with PBS (pH 7.38).

ECL test was performed in 10 mL PBS (pH 7.38, including Na2HPO4, KH2PO4 and 40 mmol/L H2O2). The scanning speed is 0.15 V/s, the working voltage is 0–0.8 V, and the voltage tube (PMT) of the photomultiplier tube is 600 V. The ECL detection process uses a three-electrode system. The system uses the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, the platinum electrode as the auxiliary electrode, and the modified GCE as the working electrode. Under the optimal conditions, the ECL intensity of different AFB1 concentrations was recorded.

To prove the successful preparation of CePO4, as shown in Fig. 1 C, the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze its crystallinity. All diffraction peaks can be easily indexed to the hexagonal CePO4 space group: P6222 (180) with cell parameters: a1 = 7.055 Å and c = 6.439 Å (JCPDS 34–1380) [43], which is very consistent with the hexagonal CePO4 crystal form. The morphology of CePO4 was characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. 1A shows that the prepared spindle-shaped CePO4 has a very uniform distribution (the length of about 1350 nm). Subsequently, the surface of the synthesized material was modified to obtain Au nanoparticles modified CePO4 (Fig. 1B). Fig. S1 UV absorption spectrum shows three characteristic peaks of CePO4@Au. The peak at 560 nm is the distinct absorption peak of gold nanoparticles [45], the distinct peaks at 256 and 273 nm are consistent with the reported data of the 4f - 5d electronic transition of Ce3+ [19]. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 1D that the prepared NHCDs have good dispersion and uniformity. The HRTEM image shows the existence of a single NHCDs with a crystal fringe pitch of 0.21 nm [20], which indicates that there is an ordered graphene-like structure in the NHCDs. Fig. S2 and Fig. 1E shows that the morphology of the BTO sample is cubic, with smooth edges and flat surfaces. Fig. 1F shows that BTO has good crystallinity and corresponds to JCPDS 05–0626. From the SEM image of BTO@Ag (Fig. S2), it can be clearly seen that there are apparent bright spots on the surface of BTO with a size of about 80 nm. Combined with the BTO Mapping of Fig. S3, it can be determined that Ag nanoparticles are successfully deposited on the BTO surface. Fig. S4 shows the characteristic UV absorption peaks of BTO@Ag-NHCDs. The distinct absorption peaks of NHCDs can be observed, which indicates that BTO@Ag and NHCDs are perfectly combined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism exploration of CePO4 and NHCDs-H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 C shows that CePO4@Au has significant catalytic effects on NHCDs. Fig. 2A and B describe the possible ECL mechanism diagram of the system we studied. There are two channels for the generation of O2 •− in this system. Above all, it is produced by the self-decomposition of H2O2 [46,47]. Next, CePO4 has high catalytic activity and electrical activity in the presence of H2O2. CePO4 can convert Ce4+into Ce3+, meanwhile, promote the generation of O2 •−. The large amount of O2 •− produced reacts with NHCDs• to make a stronger ECL signal response [48–52]. BTO@Ag has good stability and usable performance. As a carrier, NHCDs and secondary antibody markers can be effectively loaded, so that the emission effects of the luminophore are better. The possible mechanisms are as follows:

Route 1:
(1)
H 2
O 2
− e →
O 2
• −
+ 2
H
+

ECL emission:
(4)
NHCDs − e
→ NHCD
s
+
(5)
NHCD
s
+
→ NHCD
s
•
+
H
+
(6)
O 2
• −
+ NHCD
s
•
→
NHCDs * +
N 2
(7)
NHCDs *
→
NHCDs + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the construction process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the step-by-step construction process of the sensor. In the CV curve of Fig. 3A, GCE/CePO4@Au (curve a) shows a larger current peak than bare GCE (curve b), which indicates that CePO4@Au has good conductivity. However, as the electrode surface is modified with Ab1, BSA, and AFB1, and secondary antibody marker with poor conductivity, the peak current shows a downward trend, which indicates that the substances as mentioned earlier have been bound to the electrode surface layer by layer, and the proposed ECL immunosensor has been successfully prepared Eqs. (1)–((7)).

EIS is another vital measurement method to characterize the assembly process of immunosensors. The frequency range of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is vast, and it is an important method to study the dynamics of electrode process and surface phenomena. Fig. 3B is the Nyquist diagram, and it shows the response trend of the electron transfer resistance. The curve was performed in an electrolyte containing 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl. Curves (a) and (b) represent the electrochemical impedance spectra of GCE/CePO4@Au and bare GCE, respectively. Since GCE/CePO4@Au and bare GCE have good conductivity, they exhibit low impedance values. With the sequential modification of Ab1, BSA, AFB1, and secondary antibody marker, the semicircular area presents an upward trend (curves c to f). The gradual increasing of impedance values was due to the fact that the Ab1, BSA, AFB1, and secondary antibody marker could hinder the electron transfer from [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− to the electrode surface. This method further proves the successful manufacture of the proposed sensor.

Condition optimization is indispensable in ECL immunosensor detection, and it will directly affect the accuracy of the test results. The optimal conditions were selected for the concentration of CePO4@Au, the concentration of NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2, the amount of H2O2 and the pH of the test solution in turn. According to Fig. 4 A, as the concentration of CePO4@Au becomes higher, the ECL signal rises sharply, reaching the maximum at 6 mg/mL. As the concentration of CePO4@Au continues to increase, the ECL signal begin to decrease. The optimal concentration of CePO4@Au was selected as 6 mg/mL.

The higher the concentration of NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2 is, the stronger the ECL signal is. But when it reaches a certain limit, the opposite result will appear due to the self-absorption of NHCDs. If the concentration is too high, the luminescence effect will be worse. Therefore, the optimal concentration of NHCDs@BTO@Ag/Ab2 is selected as 8 mg/mL (Fig.4B).

There are two ways to consume H2O2 in this system. One way is the self-decomposition of H2O2, and another way is to react with CePO4. If the content of H2O2 is small, the two pathways will not be able to react completely, which will directly affect the luminescence effects. Because H2O2 is carcinogenic, if the content is significant, it will cause unavoidable damage to the detected object. Therefore, it is verified through rigorous experiments that the H2O2 concentration is 40 mM as the best state (as shown in Fig. 4C).

The best test conditions were selected in a wide pH range (5.29–9.18). From Fig.4D, it can be seen that the ECL signal first increases and then decreases, because the pH gets closer to neutral, and the activity of the measured substance gets higher. When the pH is a strong acid and strong base, the ECL signal is very low, which indicates that the activity and survival conditions of the tested substance have been seriously affected. The experiment proved that the pH of 7.38 is the optimal test state of the system.

After optimizing the test conditions, AFB1 was analyzed in 10 mL PBS with photomultiplier voltage (600 V), with the scanning speed of 0.15 V/s, and scanning potential from 0 to 0.8 V. A series of biosensors with AFB1 concentration (0.01 pg/mL–100 ng/mL) were incubated together to test the sensor's sensitive detection ability for the tested substance. According to 5A, as the concentration of AFB1 increases, the ECL intensity of the biosensor increases successively. As shown in Fig. 5 B, the ECL intensity trend of the calibration curve shows that the sensor has a good linear relationship with AFB1. The linear equation of the calibration curve is y = 1131.09×lg c + 10,483.84, R2 = 0.996. The detection limit is 9.55 fg/mL (S/N = 3). It is commendable that the constructed sensor is compared with some existing detection methods in Table S1. It is found that the biosensor developed by us has a wider detection range and a lower detection limit. It shows that the immunosensor has outstanding potential and practical value in bioassay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and specificity of biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of the three important indicators of stability, reproducibility and specificity is indispensable. In Fig. 5C, the sensors with the concentrations of 100 fg/mL (RSD=1.80%) and 1 ng/mL (RSD=2.43%) were randomly selected for stability testing, and it was found that the ECL intensity did not change significantly, which indicates that the constructed immunosensor is effective and the stability is good. Fig. 5D shows the ECL strength of the electrode after three weeks of storage at 4 °C, and the stability is within the acceptable range (RSD < 12.66%). Then, the ECL biosensor under the same conditions hatched with AFB1 (1 ng/mL) was detected in seven unlike batches, and the relative standard deviation was 1.93%, with good reproducibility (Fig. 5E). Several common mycotoxins, ochratoxin A (OTA), zearalenone (ZEN), zearalenone (ZEA), vomiting toxin (DON), aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), aflatoxin G1 (AFG1) and aflatoxin G2 (AFG2) were selected to interfere with the sensor. It can be seen from Fig. 5F that the interference of these mycotoxins on the sensor can be ignored. The sensor hatched with mixed samples (1 ng/mL AFB1 and 10 ng/mL interference) also showed slight variation, which indicates that the immunosensor has good selectivity. Strict verification shows that the constructed sensor has great application potential.

Accurate detection of AFB1 is essential for humans to eat healthy and sanitary food. To study the practicability and accuracy of the biosensor, the standard addition method was used to actually detect corn, rice and wheat. The treatment process is placed on the support material. When the samples were added with standard AFB1 of 0.5 ng/mL, 20 ng/mL and 100 ng/mL, the total recovery percent was in the range of 97.59–103.21%, and the relative standard deviations were all less than 4.65%. As shown in Table S2, the test results show remarkable accuracy and precision. It shows that the sensor has practical application value in the quantitative determination of AFB1 in actual samples.

In summary, CePO4@Au is a co-reaction accelerator proposed to improve the ECL efficiency of the NHCDs-H2O2 system. And it was first proposed and applied to the field of ECL immunosensor for ultra-sensitive detection of AFB1. The repeated conversion of Ce4+ and Ce3+ has high electrical and catalytic activity, which can facilitate the decomposition of H2O2 to produce O2 •−, and significantly improve the emission efficiency of the luminophore. Furthermore, modifying CePO4 on the surface of the GCE can substantially increase the effective surface area and electron transfer rate. Because BTO@Ag has good stability and availability, it can be used as a carrier to fix NHCDs and Ab2 and make the construction of the sensor more efficient and stable. The biosensor achieves ultrasensitive detection in the range of 0.01 pg/mL–100 ng/mL, with a detection limit of 9.55 fg/mL. These tactics could provide not only new angle for the manufacture of CePO4@Au to improve the ECL efficiency of the NHCDs-H2O2 system, but also new research ideas for other researchers.

All persons designated as authors should qualify for authorship, and all those who qualify should be listed. Each author should have participated sufficiently in the work to take public responsibility for appropriate portions of the content. The corresponding author should take responsibility for the integrity of the work as a whole, from inception to published article.

All persons should be based on (1) substantial contributions to conception and design, or acquisition of data, or analysis and interpretation of data; (2) drafting the article or revising it critically for important intellectual content; and (3) final approval of the version to be published. Conditions 1, 2, and 3 must all be met.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Projects of China (No. 21627809), the Jinan Scientific Research Leader Workshop Project (2018GXRC024).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.139912.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Worldwide aflatoxin contamination of agricultural products and foods: from occurrence to control
Compr. Rev. Food Sci. Food Saf.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Jallow, H. Xie, X. Tang, Z. Qi, P. Li, Worldwide aflatoxin contamination of agricultural products and foods: from occurrence to control, Compr. Rev. Food Sci. Food Saf., 20 (2021) 2332–2381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Human aflatoxicosis in developing countries: a review of toxicology, exposure, potential health consequences, and interventions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. H. Williams, T. D. Phillips, P. E. Jolly, J. K. Stiles, C. M. Jolly, A.D. Aggarwal, Human aflatoxicosis in developing countries: a review of toxicology, exposure, potential health consequences, and interventions, Am. J. Clin. Nutr., 80 (2004) 1106–1122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Changes in aflatoxins content during processing of pekmez as a traditional product of grape

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Heshmati, S. Ghadimi, A. Ranjbar, A.M. Khaneghah, Changes in aflatoxins content during processing of pekmez as a traditional product of grape, LWT Food Sci. Technol., 103 (2019) 178–185.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of nanomaterial based biosensors for detection of aflatoxin B1 toxicity in foods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Yadav, S.S. Yadav, A.K. Chhillar, J.S. Rana, An overview of nanomaterial based biosensors for detection of aflatoxin B1 toxicity in foods, Food Chem. Toxicol., 152 (2021) 112201.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The prevalence of aflatoxins in commercial baby food products: a global systematic review, meta-analysis, and risk assessment study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Bashiry, F. Javanmardi, E. Sadeghi, S. Shokri, H. Hossieni, C.A.F. Oliveira, A.
